name: Sacsayhuaman Full
description: Sacsayhuaman, a walled complex on the northern outskirts of Cusco, Peru, is a subject of intense debate and varying interpretations. Mainstream archaeology understands it as a monumental structure built primarily by the Inca civilization, starting in the 15th century, possibly on earlier foundations. It is believed to have served multiple purposes: a fortress, a ceremonial center, and a symbolic representation of Inca power. However, alternative theories, often grouped under the umbrella term 'Sacsayhuaman Full,' propose that its construction involved technologies or civilizations predating the Inca, possibly even advanced lost civilizations. These theories often focus on the precision of the stonework, the immense size of the stones (some weighing over 100 tons), and the seemingly impossible interlocking of the stones without mortar. Proponents argue that Inca tools and methods were insufficient to achieve such feats, suggesting the use of advanced technologies like sonic levitation, lost knowledge of geopolymer creation, or extraterrestrial intervention. Mainstream archaeology refutes these claims, citing extensive evidence of Inca quarrying, transportation, and construction techniques, including the use of ramps, levers, and manpower. They attribute the precision to meticulous planning, skilled labor, and iterative adjustments over decades of construction. The debate centers on the interpretation of archaeological evidence, the limitations of known Inca technology, and the plausibility of alternative explanations.
subtitle: Inca Fortress or Ancient Mystery?

claims.claim: Inca technology was insufficient to build Sacsayhuaman.
claims.evidence: The size and precision of the stones are beyond the capabilities of known Inca tools and methods.
claims.criticism: Archaeological evidence demonstrates Inca quarrying, transportation, and construction techniques using ramps, levers, and manpower. The precision is attributed to skilled labor and meticulous planning.

claims.claim: Sacsayhuaman was built using advanced technology, such as sonic levitation or geopolymer creation.
claims.evidence: The perfect interlocking of stones and the lack of mortar suggest a level of technology beyond simple tools.
claims.criticism: The interlocking stones are explained by careful shaping and fitting, and the absence of mortar is a common feature of Inca masonry. There is no credible evidence of sonic levitation or geopolymer usage.

claims.claim: Sacsayhuaman was built by a pre-Inca civilization, possibly a lost or advanced culture.
claims.evidence: Some stones appear to be from different quarries and exhibit different construction styles, suggesting multiple phases of construction or different builders.
claims.criticism: Variations in stone type and construction style can be attributed to different phases of Inca construction or the incorporation of existing structures into the complex. There is no evidence of a pre-Inca civilization capable of such monumental construction.

keyFigures.name: David Hatcher Childress
keyFigures.role: Promoter of alternative theories regarding ancient technologies.

keyFigures.name: Christopher Heaney
keyFigures.role: Academic archaeologist specializing in Andean civilizations, critical of alternative theories.

timeline.event: Battle of Sacsayhuaman during the Spanish conquest.
Ongoing debate between mainstream and alternative interpretations of Sacsayhuaman's construction.

sources.author: Hatcher Childress, D.
sources.work: Technology of the Gods: The Incredible Sciences of the Ancients.
sources.title: Hatcher Childress, D.

sources.author: Bauer, B. S.
sources.work: The Sacred Landscape of the Inca: The Cuzco Region.
sources.title: Bauer, B. S.
